config.title: Intro to Computer Science
config.description: Fundamental CS concepts: data types, algorithms, memory, and computation theory.

questions.question: Which data type is used to store a single character in most programming languages?
questions.explanation: char (short for character) is the data type designed to hold a single character value such as 'a' or '3'.

questions.question: A boolean data type can hold more than two distinct values.
questions.hint: Consider the two states of a light switch.
questions.explanation: A boolean holds exactly two values: true or false (1 or 0). It cannot represent any other state.

questions.question: What is the typical size of an int in a 32-bit system?
questions.explanation: On a 32-bit system, an int is typically 4 bytes (32 bits), allowing values from roughly -2.1 billion to 2.1 billion.

questions.question: What is the time complexity of a linear search on an array of n elements?
questions.hint: In the worst case, you check every element once.
questions.explanation: Linear search checks each element sequentially. In the worst case (element not found), it examines all n elements, giving O(n) time complexity.

questions.question: Binary search requires the input array to be:
questions.hint: The algorithm works by repeatedly halving the search space.
questions.explanation: Binary search relies on eliminating half the remaining elements each step. This only works if the array is sorted, so comparisons are meaningful.

questions.question: An algorithm with O(log n) complexity is faster than O(n) for large inputs.
questions.hint: Think about how log n grows compared to n as n increases.
questions.explanation: log n grows much slower than n. For example, log₂(1,000,000) ≈ 20, while n = 1,000,000. O(log n) algorithms scale far better for large inputs.

questions.question: Which memory type loses its contents when power is turned off?
questions.explanation: RAM (Random Access Memory) is volatile — it only retains data while powered. ROM, SSDs, and HDDs are non-volatile and persist data without power.

questions.question: What does a pointer store?

options.label: A
options.text: A copy of a variable's value

options.label: B
options.text: The memory address of another variable

questions.explanation: A pointer is a variable whose value is a memory address. It points to the location where another variable's data is stored, enabling indirect access.

questions.question: Stack memory is managed automatically by the program, while heap memory requires manual management in languages like C.
questions.hint: Local variables live on the stack; malloc() uses the heap.
questions.explanation: The call stack automatically allocates and deallocates frames as functions execute. The heap requires explicit allocation (malloc/new) and deallocation (free/delete).

questions.question: What is a compiler?

options.label: A
options.text: A program that runs source code line by line

options.label: B
options.text: A program that translates source code into machine code

options.label: C
options.text: A hardware component that executes instructions

questions.hint: It translates the entire program before execution.
questions.explanation: A compiler reads the entire source code and translates it into machine code (or bytecode) before execution. An interpreter, by contrast, translates and executes line by line.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a component of the Von Neumann architecture?
questions.hint: Von Neumann designed this in the 1940s — long before distributed ledgers.
questions.explanation: Von Neumann architecture consists of a CPU, memory unit, and I/O devices connected by a data bus. Blockchain is a modern distributed ledger concept unrelated to computer architecture basics.

questions.question: All problems can be solved by an algorithm.
questions.explanation: Turing's Halting Problem proves that some problems are undecidable — no algorithm can solve them in the general case. Computability theory defines the limits of what is algorithmically solvable.

questions.question: What is the worst-case time complexity of QuickSort?
questions.hint: This happens when the pivot is always the smallest or largest element.
questions.explanation: QuickSort's worst case (O(n²)) occurs when the pivot consistently creates maximally unbalanced partitions, such as on already-sorted input with a naive pivot strategy. Average case is O(n log n).

questions.question: In most languages, what is the result of integer division 7 / 2?
questions.explanation: When both operands are integers, most languages perform integer (truncating) division. 7 / 2 = 3.5, truncated to 3. Use floating-point types to get 3.5.

questions.question: What is a memory leak?

options.label: A
options.text: When a program reads from an invalid memory address

options.label: B
options.text: When allocated memory is never freed and accumulates over time

options.label: C
options.text: When two threads access the same memory simultaneously

options.label: D
options.text: When the stack overflows due to deep recursion

questions.hint: Memory is allocated but the reference to it is lost.
questions.explanation: A memory leak occurs when a program allocates heap memory but loses all references to it without freeing it. Over time this exhausts available memory, degrading or crashing the program.

flashcards.back: A finite, ordered set of instructions that solves a problem or performs a computation.
Random Access Memory — volatile, fast memory used by the CPU to store data currently in use.
A program that translates entire source code into machine code before execution.
Linear time — the algorithm's run time grows proportionally with the size of the input.
A variable that stores the memory address of another variable.

terminology.Algorithm: A step-by-step procedure for solving a problem in a finite number of steps.
terminology.Compiler: Translates source code into machine code before execution.
terminology.RAM: Volatile memory used by the CPU to store actively used data.
terminology.Pointer: A variable holding the memory address of another variable.
terminology.Boolean: A data type with exactly two values: true and false.
terminology.Big O Notation: A mathematical notation describing the limiting behavior of an algorithm's complexity.

achievements.title: On A Roll
achievements.description: Achieve a streak of 5 correct answers.
